en.extract:
Whyte's barbet (Stactolaema whytii) is a species of bird in the family Lybiidae (African barbets). It is found in Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania, Zambia, and Zimbabwe.
The common name and Latin binomial commemorate the naturalist Alexander Whyte, who collected in what is now Malawi.
The bird is 18–20.4 cm (7.1–8.0 in) long and weighs 51–63 g (1.8–2.2 oz). It is mainly brownish with a large black bill, head, and tail. The bird has a white malar mark, wings, and tail. There is also a small red spot under the malar mark.


Subspecies
Six subspecies are recognised:

S. w. buttoni (White, CMN, 1945) – north-central Zambia (along border with Democratic Republic of the Congo); probably also southern Democratic Republic of the Congo
S. w. stresemanni (Grote, H, 1934) – southwestern corner of Tanzania and adjacent northeastern Zambia
S. w. terminata (Clancey, PA, 1956) – highlands of southern Tanzania (Iringa region)
S. w. angoniensis (Benson, CW, 1964) – eastern Zambia to southwestern Malawi (west of Shire River)
S. w. whytii (Shelley, GE, 1893) – south-central Tanzania to southeastern Malawi and Mozambique
S. w. sowerbyi Sharpe, RB, 1898 – eastern Zimbabwe to far southern Malawi


References


External links

Whyte's barbet - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds
https://www.hbw.com/species/whytes-barbet-stactolaema-whytii

'''Whyte's barbet''' ('''''Stactolaema whytii''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Lybiidae]] (African barbets). It is found in [[Malawi]], [[Mozambique]], [[Tanzania]], [[Zambia]], and [[Zimbabwe]].

The common name and Latin binomial commemorate the naturalist Alexander Whyte, who collected in what is now Malawi.<ref>{{cite book|last=Beolens|first=Bo|title=Whose Bird? Men and Women Commemorated in the Common Names of Birds|year=2003|publisher=Christopher Helm|location=London|page=366|author2=Watkins, Michael }}</ref>

The bird is {{convert|18|–|20.4|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|51|-|63|g|oz|abbr=on}}. It is mainly brownish with a large black bill, head, and tail. The bird has a white malar mark, wings, and tail. There is also a small red spot under the malar mark.

==Subspecies==
Six [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=8 May 2026 }}</ref>
* ''S. w. buttoni'' (White, CMN, 1945) – north-central Zambia (along border with Democratic Republic of the Congo); probably also southern Democratic Republic of the Congo
* ''S. w. stresemanni'' ([[Hermann Grote|Grote, H]], 1934) – southwestern corner of Tanzania and adjacent northeastern Zambia
* ''S. w. terminata'' ([[Phillip Clancey|Clancey, PA]], 1956) – highlands of southern Tanzania (Iringa region)
* ''S. w. angoniensis'' ([[Constantine Walter Benson|Benson, CW]], 1964) – eastern Zambia to southwestern Malawi (west of [[Shire River]])
* ''S. w. whytii'' ([[George Ernest Shelley|Shelley, GE]], 1893) – south-central Tanzania to southeastern Malawi and Mozambique
* ''S. w. sowerbyi'' [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe, RB]], 1898 – eastern Zimbabwe to far southern Malawi
